theme: Recover a transducer that generalizes to longer inputs
title: The Lighthouse Relay — Recovering a Register Formula That Survives the Busy Season
strategies: trivial: predict the constant mean(train y), ignoring n and s entirely -- reproduces the checker's own constant baseline exactly (~0.1)

greedy: recognise the reading levels off (not a raw polynomial) and fit ONE pooled rational (Mobius) curve y=(n+B)/(C*n+D) to all training rows via cross-multiply linearisation -- a real step up from naive curve-fitting, bounded and correctly shaped, but ignores the gear column s entirely; since the 3 gears have different coefficients, one pooled curve is a single compromise asymptote that is systematically wrong for whichever gears its curve doesn't happen to match, and training's modest ship counts never expose that gear-by-count interaction

strong: split the training rows by gear s FIRST, then apply the SAME cross-multiply linearisation separately per gear (each gear's ~30 rows exactly determine its own Mobius map up to scale), and combine the three recovered curves into one expression via a degree-2 Lagrange selector on s (no branching syntax needed) -- a rational map recovered from finitely many exact points is the SAME function at any n, so it extrapolates correctly no matter how far the busy season pushes the ship count

headroom above strong: robust/iterative reweighting of the per-gear linear systems against training noise, or exploiting near-degenerate curvature within a gear's training range (when a gear's knee point sits well beyond n=90) to sharpen the recovered asymptote toward the sensor-noise floor
